title: Quiz: one big table vs related tables
prompt_md: What is the core tradeoff between keeping everything in one big table versus splitting data into related tables joined by foreign keys?
options: Normalizing into related tables avoids duplication and protects integrity but needs joins; denormalizing into fewer/bigger tables avoids joins for faster reads but duplicates data that can drift — you pick per access pattern
One big table is always faster and uses less storage, so it is the right default
Normalization and denormalization produce identical performance, so the choice is purely stylistic
Splitting tables is always wrong because joins are too slow to ever use
explanation_md: **Normalization** stores each fact exactly once across related tables linked by **foreign keys** — this minimizes duplication and preserves **referential integrity**, at the cost of needing **joins** to reassemble data. **Denormalization** deliberately duplicates or pre-combines data to avoid those joins and speed up **reads**, accepting that the copies can **drift out of sync** and that writes get heavier. There's no universal winner: you choose based on the **access pattern** — normalize for write-heavy/transactional integrity, denormalize for read-heavy hot paths. Saying "it depends on how the data is read" is exactly the judgment interviewers want.
